enrichment.short_description: Timestripe is a goal-tracking and productivity app that visualizes tasks, habits and long-term goals across a scrollable timeline spanning days to decades.
enrichment.seo_title: Timestripe Review: Pricing, Features and Alternatives 2026
enrichment.seo_meta_description: See what Timestripe costs, its Horizon timeline view, Climbs habit tracker, and how it compares to other goal-setting and productivity apps.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Timestripe is a goal management and productivity application built by a small independent team originally based in Saint Petersburg and now incorporated as Timestripe OU in Estonia. Founded in 2016 by Sergey Kulinkovich and Andrew Maykov, the product grew out of a desire to combine calendar planning, to-do lists and long-range goal tracking into one continuous timeline rather than the day-by-day view used by most task managers. Its signature feature, the Horizon view, lets users zoom out from a single day to a week, month, quarter, year or multi-year span, showing how daily tasks ladder up to bigger objectives. A companion feature called Climbs breaks large goals into incremental, trackable steps so users can build habits gradually instead of committing to all-or-nothing routines. Unfinished daily tasks automatically roll forward to the next day so nothing gets silently dropped. Timestripe also includes shared to-do lists and notes for lightweight team collaboration, plus native web and mobile apps that sync across devices. Pricing follows a freemium model: a free Basic plan covers core calendar, task and note functionality, while the Pro plan, around 5 US dollars per month when billed annually, unlocks unlimited Horizons, advanced customization and priority support for individual power users. A Team plan with custom, seat-based pricing adds shared workspaces for small teams and organizations. Timestripe positions itself against general task managers like Todoist and Things, and against goal-tracking apps like Sunsama, by emphasizing the timeline-first visualization rather than a flat list of tasks. It is best suited to individuals who think in terms of long-range personal or professional goals, such as students, freelancers and founders, rather than large enterprise teams needing deep project-management or Gantt-chart functionality, which Timestripe does not provide.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Timestripe
paragraphs: Timestripe is a cross-platform goal management and productivity app that reframes task management around a scrollable timeline instead of a single day's list. Rather than showing only what is due today, Timestripe's Horizon view lets a user pull back to see a week, a month, a quarter, a year or even a multi-year span at once, with tasks, notes and goals arranged along that continuum. The idea is to make it easier to see how a task completed today actually contributes to a goal set for next year, something flat to-do list apps struggle to visualize.

The app was built by a small independent team founded in 2016 and is now incorporated as Timestripe OU in Estonia. It is available as a web app and native mobile apps, with data synced across devices. Rather than competing purely on task-completion speed, Timestripe markets itself primarily to individuals doing long-range personal planning, such as students mapping out a degree, freelancers plotting a business quarter, or anyone using a bullet-journal-style approach to their year.

Timestripe complements its timeline view with lightweight collaboration tools, including shared to-do lists and shared notes, so small teams or partners can coordinate on a subset of goals without needing full project-management software.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Timestripe's core feature is the Horizon, a zoomable timeline that shows the same workspace at multiple time scales, from a single day up to a multi-year view. Users can drag tasks between horizons, so a task assigned to 'this quarter' can later be broken down into weekly or daily action items without re-entering the underlying information.

Climbs is Timestripe's habit- and goal-building feature. Instead of marking a goal as simply done or not done, a Climb lets a user define incremental steps or repeated sessions that count toward a larger objective, which is intended to make ambitious goals feel achievable in smaller pieces.

The app also includes automatic task rollover, so any task left incomplete at the end of a day is automatically carried to the next day rather than silently disappearing, along with shared lists and notes for basic team coordination, and calendar integrations for viewing external events alongside Timestripe goals.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Timestripe uses a freemium model. The free Basic plan includes the core Horizon timeline, task management and notes for individual use. The paid Pro plan, listed at roughly 5 US dollars per month when billed annually, removes limits on the number of active Horizons and unlocks additional customization and priority support.

For teams, Timestripe offers a separate Team plan with pricing based on team size and required seats rather than a flat published rate; prospective team customers are directed to contact Timestripe directly for a quote.

features.title: Horizon Timeline View
features.description: Zoom between daily, weekly, monthly, quarterly and multi-year views of the same tasks and goals to see how short-term work ladders up to long-term objectives.

features.title: Climbs Goal Tracker
features.description: Break large goals into incremental steps or repeated sessions so progress can be tracked gradually instead of as a single pass or fail milestone.

features.title: Automatic Task Rollover
features.description: Incomplete tasks automatically move to the next day so nothing gets lost between planning sessions.

features.title: Shared Lists and Notes
features.description: Create shared to-do lists and notes so small teams or partners can coordinate on specific goals without switching to separate project-management software.

features.title: Cross-Platform Sync
features.description: Web app and native mobile apps keep tasks, notes and Horizons in sync across devices.

features.title: Calendar Integration
features.description: View external calendar events alongside Timestripe tasks and goals for a single combined schedule.

features.title: Customizable Workspace
features.description: Adjust themes, layouts and Horizon groupings to match personal or team planning styles.

pros: Unique timeline-based visualization makes long-range goal planning more intuitive than flat to-do lists
Climbs feature helps break down large, intimidating goals into manageable steps
Free Basic plan covers core functionality for individual users
Affordable Pro plan compared to many productivity and goal-tracking competitors
Available on both web and mobile with cross-device sync
cons: Lacks the deep project-management features, such as Gantt charts and dependencies, found in dedicated PM tools
Team collaboration features are lighter than dedicated team task managers like Asana or ClickUp
Team plan pricing is not published and requires contacting sales
Smaller company and community than larger productivity app competitors, which may affect long-term feature velocity
The timeline-first interface has a learning curve for users used to conventional flat to-do lists

faqs.question: What is Timestripe used for?
faqs.answer: Timestripe is used for personal and team goal management, combining a scrollable timeline, task lists, notes and habit tracking into a single workspace so users can plan across days, months and years.

faqs.question: Is Timestripe free?
faqs.answer: Yes. Timestripe offers a free Basic plan that covers the core Horizon timeline, tasks and notes. A paid Pro plan adds unlimited Horizons and extra customization for individual users, and a separate Team plan is available with custom pricing.

faqs.question: How much does Timestripe Pro cost?
faqs.answer: The Pro plan is priced at approximately 5 US dollars per month when billed annually. Monthly billing without an annual commitment is typically priced somewhat higher.

faqs.question: Does Timestripe have a mobile app?
faqs.answer: Yes, Timestripe is available as a web app and as native mobile apps that sync tasks, notes and Horizons across devices.

faqs.question: What is the Horizon feature in Timestripe?
faqs.answer: Horizon is Timestripe's signature zoomable timeline that shows the same tasks and goals at different time scales, from a single day up to a multi-year view, so users can see how daily work connects to long-term objectives.

faqs.question: Who founded Timestripe?
faqs.answer: Timestripe was founded in 2016 by Sergey Kulinkovich and Andrew Maykov and is now incorporated as Timestripe OU in Estonia.

faqs.question: What is a Climb in Timestripe?
faqs.answer: A Climb is Timestripe's goal-tracking mechanism that breaks a large goal into incremental steps or repeated sessions, letting users track gradual progress rather than a single completion checkbox.

faqs.question: Is Timestripe good for teams?
faqs.answer: Timestripe supports basic team collaboration through shared lists and notes, and offers a dedicated Team plan, though it is primarily designed for individual long-range planning rather than full team project management.

use_cases.title: Long-term personal goal planning
use_cases.description: Individuals use Timestripe's Horizon view to connect daily to-dos with quarterly and yearly goals, making it easier to stay oriented toward long-range objectives.

use_cases.title: Habit building through incremental steps
use_cases.description: Users lean on the Climbs feature to break intimidating goals like fitness targets or learning a new skill into smaller repeated actions that are tracked over time.
use_cases.audience: Individuals building new habits or routines

use_cases.title: Lightweight shared planning for small teams
use_cases.description: Small teams or partners use shared lists and notes inside Timestripe to coordinate on a limited set of shared goals without adopting full project-management software.

enrichment.short_description: TinaCMS is an open-source, Git-backed headless CMS with live visual editing for React frameworks like Next.js, storing content as Markdown or MDX directly in a GitHub repository.
enrichment.headquarters: Neutral Bay, New South Wales, Australia
enrichment.seo_title: TinaCMS Review 2026: Pricing, Features and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: TinaCMS is a Git-backed headless CMS with live visual editing. Compare TinaCMS pricing plans, key features and how it stacks up against other CMS tools.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: TinaCMS is an open-source, Git-backed headless content management system built for developers using React-based frameworks such as Next.js and Gatsby. It was released publicly in October 2019 as the successor to Forestry.io, a closed-source Git-backed CMS that launched in 2016; the original Forestry.io team built TinaCMS to add real-time, inline visual editing on top of a similar Git-as-database architecture. In May 2024, Australian software consultancy SSW (Superior Software for Windows) acquired TinaCMS and now maintains and develops the product. TinaCMS's core differentiator is that content edits happen directly on the live rendered page rather than in a separate admin form, and every save is committed as a Git commit to the site's own GitHub repository, so content is stored as Markdown or MDX files alongside code rather than in a proprietary database. This makes it attractive to teams that want a CMS for static or JAMstack sites without giving up version control, branching, or the ability to self-host. TinaCMS operates a hybrid open-source-plus-SaaS model: the core editing framework is free and open source and can be self-hosted, while TinaCloud is the commercial, hosted layer that adds real-time collaboration, editorial workflows, role-based access and managed authentication. TinaCloud pricing starts with a free tier for two users, then scales through Team, around 24 US dollars per month, Team Plus, around 41 US dollars per month, and Business, around 249 US dollars per month, with custom Enterprise pricing for larger organizations needing SSO and dedicated support. TinaCMS competes with other Git-based and headless CMS options such as Contentful, Sanity, Decap CMS and Strapi, distinguishing itself through its emphasis on true inline visual editing rather than a separate content dashboard.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is TinaCMS
paragraphs: TinaCMS is an open-source headless content management system designed for developers building sites with React-based frameworks such as Next.js, Gatsby and Astro. Unlike traditional headless CMS platforms that store content in a proprietary database accessed through an API, TinaCMS treats a Git repository as the source of truth, saving content as Markdown or MDX files that live alongside a project's code and are versioned through normal Git commits.

The project traces back to Forestry.io, a closed-source Git-backed CMS launched in 2016. The same team later built TinaCMS as an open-source, more flexible successor with a focus on live visual editing, releasing it publicly in October 2019. In May 2024, Australian software consultancy SSW acquired TinaCMS, taking over its ongoing development, support and commercial hosting offering, TinaCloud.

TinaCMS's central idea is inline, real-time editing: instead of editing content in a separate admin panel and then previewing the result, editors make changes directly on the rendered page and see the update instantly, while the underlying Markdown or MDX file is updated and can be committed to Git. This appeals to teams who want a comfortable content-editing experience for marketers or writers without sacrificing the developer benefits of Git-based version control.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: TinaCMS's signature capability is visual, inline editing: content fields are mapped to a schema defined in code, and editors can click directly into text, images or structured fields on the live page to make changes, with edits reflected immediately in the preview. This removes the disconnect between a CMS admin form and the actual rendered output that many headless CMS tools suffer from.

Because content is stored as Markdown or MDX in the project's own Git repository, every edit becomes a Git commit, giving teams full version history, branching and pull-request based review for content changes just like code changes. This also means content can be edited locally, in a code editor, or through the TinaCMS visual interface interchangeably.

TinaCloud, the hosted commercial layer, adds real-time multi-user collaboration, an editorial workflow with draft and review states, role-based permissions, and managed authentication so non-technical teams can log in and edit without needing GitHub accounts themselves. Enterprise customers can add single sign-on and custom roles.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: TinaCMS itself, the open-source editing framework, is free to self-host with no user limits imposed by the software. Most of TinaCMS's monetization comes through TinaCloud, the optional hosted backend that handles authentication, real-time collaboration and media management without requiring a team to run its own infrastructure.

TinaCloud's free tier supports up to two users per project. Paid tiers scale from the Team plan, around 24 US dollars per month or 290 US dollars per year, through Team Plus and Business tiers that raise user limits and add features like editorial workflows and API access, up to custom Enterprise pricing for organizations that need SSO, custom roles and dedicated support.

features.title: Live Visual Editing
features.description: Edit content fields directly on the rendered page instead of in a separate admin form, with changes reflected instantly in the live preview.

features.title: Git-Backed Content Storage
features.description: Content is saved as Markdown or MDX files in the project's own GitHub repository, giving teams full version history and branching for content just like code.

features.title: Framework Support
features.description: Purpose-built for React-based frameworks including Next.js, Gatsby and Astro, with a schema-driven content model defined in code.

features.title: TinaCloud Hosting
features.description: Optional managed backend that adds authentication, real-time collaboration and media management without self-hosting infrastructure.

features.title: Editorial Workflow
features.description: Draft and review states let editorial teams stage content changes before they are merged and published.

features.title: Role-Based Access Control
features.description: Assign roles and permissions to control who can edit, review or publish specific content types.

features.title: Open-Source Core
features.description: The core CMS framework is free and open source, allowing teams to self-host and fully customize the editing experience.

pros: Content stays in the team's own Git repository rather than a proprietary vendor database, avoiding lock-in
True inline visual editing gives non-technical editors a comfortable, WYSIWYG-like experience
Free self-hosted option with no user limits for teams comfortable managing their own infrastructure
Strong fit for JAMstack and static-site workflows already using Next.js or Gatsby
Editorial workflow and role-based access make it viable for larger content teams via TinaCloud
cons: Primarily built for React-based frameworks, so it is a weaker fit for non-React stacks
Requires developer setup to define the content schema before non-technical editors can use it
Free TinaCloud tier is capped at two users, so most real teams need a paid plan
Git-based content model has a learning curve for editors unfamiliar with version control concepts
Recent ownership change following the SSW acquisition introduces some uncertainty about the open-source project's long-term roadmap

faqs.question: What is TinaCMS?
faqs.answer: TinaCMS is an open-source, Git-backed headless CMS that lets teams edit content directly on the live rendered page while storing the underlying content as Markdown or MDX files in a GitHub repository.

faqs.question: Is TinaCMS free?
faqs.answer: The core TinaCMS editing framework is free and open source for self-hosting. TinaCloud, the optional managed hosting and collaboration layer, has a free tier for two users and paid plans starting around 24 US dollars per month.

faqs.question: What is the difference between TinaCMS and Forestry.io?
faqs.answer: Forestry.io was a closed-source, Git-backed CMS launched in 2016 by the same team that later built TinaCMS as an open-source, more extensible successor with live inline editing, released in 2019. Forestry.io has since been sunset in favor of TinaCMS.

faqs.question: Who owns TinaCMS now?
faqs.answer: Australian software consultancy SSW acquired TinaCMS in May 2024 and now maintains its development, support and TinaCloud hosting service.

faqs.question: Does TinaCMS work with Next.js?
faqs.answer: Yes, TinaCMS is built primarily for React-based frameworks, with strong support for Next.js as well as Gatsby and Astro.

faqs.question: How is content stored in TinaCMS?
faqs.answer: Content is stored as Markdown or MDX files inside the project's own Git repository, so every edit is tracked as a normal Git commit rather than stored in a separate database.

faqs.question: What is TinaCloud?
faqs.answer: TinaCloud is TinaCMS's optional commercial hosted backend that adds authentication, real-time multi-user collaboration, editorial workflows and media management on top of the open-source editing framework.

faqs.question: Is TinaCMS good for non-technical content editors?
faqs.answer: Yes, once a developer sets up the content schema, TinaCMS's live inline editing gives non-technical editors a page-based, visual editing experience comparable to traditional CMS admin panels.

use_cases.title: JAMstack marketing sites with a Git-based workflow
use_cases.description: Development teams building marketing or documentation sites on Next.js or Gatsby use TinaCMS to let content editors update copy without needing to write Markdown by hand or use a separate database-backed CMS.
use_cases.audience: Frontend developers and marketing teams on JAMstack sites

use_cases.title: Documentation sites with reviewable content changes
use_cases.description: Teams maintaining developer documentation use TinaCMS's Git-backed storage so content edits go through the same pull-request review process as code changes.
use_cases.audience: Developer relations and technical writing teams

use_cases.title: Agencies building client-editable static sites
use_cases.description: Agencies use TinaCMS and TinaCloud to hand off statically generated sites to clients who need to edit copy and images themselves without touching code or a GitHub account.

features: Up to 20 users, expandable to unlimited

enrichment.short_description: Tine is an open-source groupware, CRM and collaboration suite covering email, calendar, contacts, tasks and CRM, developed by German company Metaways Infosystems and released under the AGPLv3 license.
enrichment.seo_title: Tine Groupware Review: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Tine is an open-source groupware and CRM suite with email, calendar and tasks. See Tine's pricing, self-hosting options and how it compares to alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Tine, previously called Tine 2.0, is an open-source business collaboration suite covering groupware, CRM and light ERP functionality, developed and maintained by German software company Metaways Infosystems GmbH, founded in Hamburg in 2001. The project began in 2007 out of discussions within the eGroupware open-source community; two Metaways-funded developers built an initial MySQL-backed implementation with access control lists and a new interface, and the first version launched on October 30, 2008. The name Tine 2.0 was originally a playful backronym for This is not eGroupWare, signaling that it was an independent rewrite rather than a fork of the earlier eGroupware project. The suite is released under the GNU Affero General Public License version 3 (AGPLv3), and unlike many commercial open-source products that hold back premium features behind a closed enterprise edition, Tine publishes the complete source code of all standard applications and features as open source, including the modules used in its Business Edition. Tine's server is written in PHP with a SQL database backend, and its client runs as a JavaScript single-page application in the browser. Core modules include email, shared calendars, address books, task management, file sync and share, time tracking and a built-in CRM, making it comparable to products like Zimbra, Zoho, or the Nextcloud groupware modules, but oriented toward organizations that want full source-code access and self-hosting control. Organizations can self-host Tine at no license cost, or subscribe to Metaways's hosted SaaS offering, which starts at roughly 12.90 euros per month for a small package of user accounts, email inboxes and storage, with additional users and storage billed incrementally. Commercial partners also offer paid support and maintenance for the self-hosted Business Edition. Note: this Tine is the open-source groupware and CRM suite from the tine20.org lineage, unrelated to any person or brand of the same name.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Tine
paragraphs: Tine, formerly branded Tine 2.0, is an open-source groupware, CRM and business collaboration suite developed by German software company Metaways Infosystems GmbH. It bundles the applications most organizations rely on for daily collaboration, including shared email, calendars, address books and task management, alongside a built-in CRM module, into a single self-hosted or hosted web application.

The project originated from discussions within the eGroupware open-source community in 2007. Metaways funded two developers to build a new MySQL-backed implementation with access control lists and a redesigned interface, and released the first public version on October 30, 2008. The name Tine 2.0 was a playful nod to This is not eGroupWare, emphasizing that it was a fresh, independent rewrite rather than a fork of the existing eGroupware codebase.

Tine is released under the GNU Affero General Public License version 3 (AGPLv3), and its developers publish the full source code of every standard application, including those bundled in the commercially supported Business Edition, rather than reserving features for a closed enterprise tier. This makes it a fully open-source alternative to proprietary groupware suites for organizations that want to self-host and audit their collaboration software.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Tine's core groupware modules cover shared email over IMAP, calendars, address books and task management, all accessible through a browser-based JavaScript client backed by a PHP and SQL server. These modules are designed to work together, so a calendar event can be linked to a contact or a task can be tied to a CRM record.

Beyond basic groupware, Tine includes a built-in CRM module for tracking leads, contacts and sales activity, plus optional modules for file sync and share, time tracking, and light project or ticket management, positioning it as a broader business collaboration platform rather than just an email and calendar tool.

Because the complete source is AGPLv3-licensed, organizations can self-host Tine on their own infrastructure with no per-user licensing fee, audit or modify the code, and choose from independent commercial partners for paid support, or subscribe directly to Metaways's own hosted SaaS packages for a managed alternative.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Tine's self-hosted Business Edition is free to download, install and run under the AGPLv3 license, with no per-user or per-feature license cost; commercial partners offer paid support and maintenance contracts for organizations that want guaranteed response times.

Metaways also runs a directly hosted SaaS version with tiered monthly packages: the base Groupware package starts at roughly 12.90 euros per month, excluding VAT, for a small number of user accounts, email inboxes and storage, with additional users and storage billed incrementally, and higher packages, including Pro and a Sync and Share add-on, priced separately for teams that want extra functionality.

features.title: Shared Email, Calendar and Contacts
features.description: Core groupware modules for IMAP email, shared calendars and address books that keep a team synchronized on schedules and contact information.

features.title: Built-In CRM
features.description: A native CRM module for tracking leads, contacts and sales activity without needing a separate third-party CRM system.

features.title: Task and Project Management
features.description: Assign, track and organize tasks and light projects alongside the rest of the groupware suite.

features.title: File Sync and Share
features.description: An optional module for syncing and sharing files across a team, similar to a self-hosted cloud storage layer.

features.title: Full Open-Source Source Code
features.description: All standard applications, including those in the Business Edition, are published as open source under AGPLv3 rather than held back for a closed enterprise tier.

features.title: Self-Hosting Option
features.description: Organizations can install Tine on their own servers for full data control, with no per-user license fee.

features.title: Managed SaaS Hosting
features.description: Metaways offers a directly hosted, subscription-based version of Tine for teams that prefer not to manage their own infrastructure.

pros: Fully open-source under AGPLv3, including features normally reserved for paid enterprise tiers elsewhere
Combines groupware and CRM in one suite, reducing the need for separate tools
Free to self-host with no per-user licensing cost
Long track record, with continuous development since 2008
Managed hosting option available for teams that do not want to self-administer a server
cons: Smaller community and ecosystem compared to mainstream groupware platforms like Zimbra or Nextcloud
Interface and documentation are less polished than large commercial competitors
Self-hosting requires PHP and SQL server administration skills
Primarily documented and supported in German and English, which may limit reach in other markets
Hosted SaaS pricing scales per user and per gigabyte, which can add up for larger teams

faqs.question: What is Tine?
faqs.answer: Tine, formerly Tine 2.0, is an open-source groupware, CRM and business collaboration suite that bundles email, calendar, contacts, tasks and CRM into one web application, developed by Metaways Infosystems GmbH.

faqs.question: Is Tine free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Tine's self-hosted Business Edition is free to download and run under the AGPLv3 license. Metaways also offers a paid hosted SaaS version with monthly pricing that starts around 12.90 euros per month.

faqs.question: What does the name Tine 2.0 mean?
faqs.answer: Tine 2.0 was originally a playful backronym for This is not eGroupWare, signaling that the project was an independent new codebase rather than a fork of the earlier eGroupware suite it grew out of discussing.

faqs.question: Who develops Tine?
faqs.answer: Tine is developed and maintained by Metaways Infosystems GmbH, a software company founded in 2001 and headquartered in Hamburg, Germany.

faqs.question: Does Tine include a CRM?
faqs.answer: Yes, Tine includes a built-in CRM module for tracking contacts, leads and sales activity alongside its groupware features.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Tine?
faqs.answer: Yes, Tine can be self-hosted on your own PHP and SQL server infrastructure at no license cost, since the full Business Edition source code is published under AGPLv3.

faqs.question: What license does Tine use?
faqs.answer: Tine is released under the GNU Affero General Public License version 3 (AGPLv3).

faqs.question: How does Tine compare to eGroupware?
faqs.answer: Tine originated from discussions within the eGroupware community but was built as an independent rewrite rather than a fork, with its own MySQL-backed architecture and interface first released in 2008.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted email and calendar collaboration
use_cases.description: Organizations that want full control over their email and calendar data self-host Tine on their own servers instead of relying on a third-party SaaS provider.

use_cases.title: Combined groupware and CRM for small businesses
use_cases.description: Small and mid-sized businesses use Tine's built-in CRM alongside its groupware modules to manage both internal collaboration and customer relationships in one system.

use_cases.title: Open-source alternative to proprietary groupware
use_cases.description: Organizations that require fully auditable, open-source software for compliance or budget reasons choose Tine over closed-source suites like Microsoft Exchange or Zimbra's proprietary tiers.

enrichment.short_description: Tiny File Manager is a free, open-source, single-file PHP web-based file manager that lets you browse, upload, edit and manage files on a server through the browser without a database.
enrichment.seo_title: Tiny File Manager: Free Open-Source PHP File Manager
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Tiny File Manager is a free single-file PHP web file manager. See its features, GitHub project, code editor, and how it compares to other file managers.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Tiny File Manager is a free, open-source, web-based file manager built as a single PHP file, created by developer Prasath Mani and first published on GitHub on July 5, 2017. It has no database dependency and no complex installation process: a user copies the single tinyfilemanager.php file, renaming it is recommended for security, to a web server, and it immediately provides a browser-based interface for uploading, downloading, editing, deleting, renaming, copying, moving, previewing and compressing or extracting files and folders. As of mid-2026 the project has accumulated roughly six thousand stars on GitHub and is licensed under the GPL-3.0 open-source license, meaning it is free to use, modify and redistribute with no cost or vendor lock-in. Its most notable built-in feature is an embedded Cloud9-based code editor with syntax highlighting for more than 150 programming languages and over 35 color themes, letting developers or site administrators edit code files directly in the browser rather than downloading and re-uploading them. It also includes basic user authentication with configurable per-user permissions and folder access restrictions, support for more than 50 interface languages, and a responsive layout that works on desktop, tablet and mobile devices. Because it requires only PHP and no external services, Tiny File Manager is commonly used by developers and sysadmins as a lightweight alternative to full-featured commercial file management panels, or as an emergency file browser when SSH or FTP access is unavailable, on shared hosting, small VPS instances, or self-hosted home server setups such as those built with Docker. It has no formal company or pricing model behind it; it is maintained as a community open-source project on GitHub, distinct from unrelated similarly named tools like TinaCMS, which is a headless content management system.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Tiny File Manager
paragraphs: Tiny File Manager is a free, open-source web-based file manager distributed as a single PHP file, created by developer Prasath Mani and first published on GitHub in July 2017. Unlike most file management software, it requires no database, no framework and almost no configuration: copying one PHP file to a web server is enough to get a working file browser accessible from any browser.

The project is released under the GPL-3.0 open-source license and has grown to roughly six thousand GitHub stars, making it one of the more widely used lightweight, self-hosted file managers in the PHP ecosystem. It is maintained as a community project rather than by a commercial company, with contributions accepted through GitHub.

Because of its minimal footprint, Tiny File Manager is popular among developers, sysadmins and hobbyists who need a quick way to browse and manage files on a server that does not already have a full file management panel installed, including shared hosting environments, small VPS instances and self-hosted home server setups.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The core feature set covers standard file operations performed through a browser: upload, download, rename, copy, move, delete, and create new files and folders, along with previewing images, video and text files directly in the interface.

Tiny File Manager includes a built-in code editor powered by Cloud9, supporting syntax highlighting for more than 150 programming languages and over 35 visual themes, so developers can edit configuration files or scripts directly in the browser without a separate FTP or SSH session.

It also supports compressing files into zip or tar archives and extracting existing archives, basic multi-user authentication with configurable folder-level permissions, and a translation system covering more than 50 interface languages, all while remaining responsive on desktop, tablet and mobile screens.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Tiny File Manager is completely free and open source under the GPL-3.0 license, with no paid tier, subscription or usage limits. Anyone can download, modify and redistribute the code at no cost.
Because it has no vendor or hosted service behind it, there are no pricing plans to compare; the only cost is the hosting environment the user already runs it on.

features.title: Single-File PHP Installation
features.description: The entire application ships as one PHP file that can be copied to any PHP-enabled web server with no database or framework dependencies.

features.title: Built-In Cloud9 Code Editor
features.description: Edit text and code files directly in the browser with syntax highlighting for over 150 languages and more than 35 themes.

features.title: File Compression and Extraction
features.description: Compress files into zip or tar archives, or extract existing archives, directly from the file manager interface.

features.title: Multi-User Authentication
features.description: Create multiple user accounts with configurable folder-level permissions and access restrictions.

features.title: 50+ Language Translations
features.description: The interface supports more than 50 languages through an included translation system.

features.title: Responsive Design
features.description: The interface adapts to desktop, tablet and mobile screens for file management from any device.

features.title: File Preview
features.description: Preview images, videos and text files directly in the browser without downloading them first.

pros: Completely free and open source with no licensing cost
Extremely simple to install, requiring only a single PHP file
Built-in code editor eliminates the need for separate FTP plus text editor workflows
Actively maintained on GitHub with a large community and thousands of stars
No database dependency, keeping the footprint and attack surface small
cons: Being a single publicly known PHP file, default installations are a common target for attackers if not renamed and secured properly
Lacks enterprise features like audit logging, granular API access or SSO found in commercial file management platforms
No official managed hosting or paid support option
Interface is functional but visually basic compared to modern commercial file managers
Relies on the security of the underlying PHP hosting environment, so misconfiguration can expose sensitive files

faqs.question: What is Tiny File Manager?
faqs.answer: Tiny File Manager is a free, open-source web-based file manager built as a single PHP file that lets you upload, edit, delete and manage files on a web server through your browser.

faqs.question: Is Tiny File Manager free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it is completely free and open source under the GPL-3.0 license, with no paid tiers or usage limits.

faqs.question: Does Tiny File Manager require a database?
faqs.answer: No, Tiny File Manager has no database dependency. It only requires a PHP-enabled web server to run.

faqs.question: Who created Tiny File Manager?
faqs.answer: Tiny File Manager was created by developer Prasath Mani and first published on GitHub in July 2017.

faqs.question: Does Tiny File Manager have a code editor?
faqs.answer: Yes, it includes a built-in Cloud9-based code editor with syntax highlighting for more than 150 programming languages and over 35 themes.

faqs.question: Is Tiny File Manager secure?
faqs.answer: It includes basic authentication and configurable folder permissions, but because it is a well-known single PHP file, administrators are advised to rename the file and add extra server-level protection to reduce exposure.

faqs.question: Can Tiny File Manager compress and extract files?
faqs.answer: Yes, it supports compressing files into zip or tar archives and extracting existing archives directly from the browser interface.

faqs.question: Is Tiny File Manager the same as TinaCMS?
faqs.answer: No. Tiny File Manager is a standalone lightweight PHP file browser, while TinaCMS is an unrelated headless content management system for React-based websites.

use_cases.title: Emergency file access on shared hosting
use_cases.description: Developers use Tiny File Manager as a fallback way to browse and edit files on shared hosting when FTP or SSH access is unavailable or inconvenient.
use_cases.audience: Web developers on shared or budget hosting

use_cases.title: Lightweight file browser for self-hosted servers
use_cases.description: Homelab and self-hosting enthusiasts deploy Tiny File Manager alongside Docker containers to get a simple file browsing and editing interface without installing a full server panel.

use_cases.title: Quick server-side code edits
use_cases.description: Sysadmins use the built-in code editor to make fast configuration or script edits directly on a server without opening a separate SSH session.

enrichment.short_description: Tiny Tiny RSS (tt-rss) is a free, open-source, self-hosted web-based RSS and Atom feed reader and aggregator that lets users control their own news feeds instead of relying on a third-party service.
enrichment.seo_title: Tiny Tiny RSS (tt-rss): Free Self-Hosted RSS Reader
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Tiny Tiny RSS is a free, open-source, self-hosted RSS and Atom feed reader. See its features, plugins, Docker setup and community fork status.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Tiny Tiny RSS, commonly abbreviated tt-rss, is a free and open-source, self-hosted RSS and Atom feed reader and aggregator written in PHP, originally created by developer Andrew Dolgov starting in 2005. Unlike hosted feed readers such as Feedly or Inoreader, tt-rss runs entirely on infrastructure the user controls, typically deployed via Docker and Docker Compose, so the user's reading data and subscription list are never stored on a third party's servers. It is licensed under the GNU General Public License version 3 (GPLv3) and has long been one of the most widely deployed self-hosted RSS readers in the open-source and privacy-focused hosting community, commonly listed alongside alternatives like FreshRSS and Miniflux. Core features include support for RSS, Atom and other feed formats, OPML import and export for migrating subscriptions from other readers, customizable keyboard shortcuts for fast keyboard-driven reading, article sharing to social platforms, a plugin system for extending functionality, and an official Android client for reading feeds on mobile. In a significant change to the project, the original tt-rss.org site and its founder-led development were retired on November 1, 2025, and the project has continued as a community-run fork on GitHub, created by a long-time contributor on October 3, 2025, with a stated goal of continuing development without major breaking changes. Because it is entirely open source and free to self-host, tt-rss has no pricing plans or company behind it; anyone with a server capable of running PHP and a database can install it at no cost, making it a durable option for readers who want long-term control over their RSS consumption rather than depending on a commercial hosted service that could shut down or change its pricing.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Tiny Tiny RSS
paragraphs: Tiny Tiny RSS, widely known by its abbreviation tt-rss, is a free and open-source, self-hosted RSS and Atom feed reader written in PHP. It was originally created by developer Andrew Dolgov, with copyright dating back to 2005, and has since become one of the most established self-hosted feed readers in the open-source community.

Unlike cloud-based feed readers such as Feedly or Inoreader, tt-rss runs entirely on a server the user controls, whether a home server, a VPS or a shared host, and is most commonly deployed with Docker and Docker Compose. This means subscription lists, read and unread state and saved articles stay on infrastructure the user owns rather than a third-party company's servers.

The project underwent a significant transition in late 2025: the original tt-rss.org site and its founder-led development were retired on November 1, 2025, and the project now continues as a community-maintained fork hosted on GitHub, created on October 3, 2025 by a long-time contributor, with the stated intent of continuing development without major breaking changes for existing users.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: tt-rss aggregates RSS, Atom and other feed formats into a single unified reading interface, with support for OPML import and export so users can migrate their existing subscriptions from another reader in a single step.

The interface supports customizable keyboard shortcuts for fast, keyboard-driven navigation between feeds and articles, a hallmark feature that has made it popular among power users who read a high volume of feeds daily.

tt-rss includes article sharing to various social platforms, RSS feed export of saved or starred articles, an extensible plugin system for adding custom functionality, including full-text content extraction plugins for feeds that only publish summaries, and an official Android client for reading feeds on mobile devices.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Tiny Tiny RSS is entirely free and open source under the GNU General Public License version 3 (GPLv3). There is no paid tier, subscription or usage cap; the only cost is whatever server or hosting the user runs it on.

Because there is no vendor selling tt-rss as a hosted service, some third-party providers offer paid managed tt-rss hosting for users who do not want to self-administer a server, but this is separate from the open-source project itself.

features.title: RSS and Atom Aggregation
features.description: Subscribe to and read RSS, Atom and other feed formats in a single unified interface.

features.title: OPML Import and Export
features.description: Migrate subscription lists in or out of tt-rss in a single step using the standard OPML format.

features.title: Keyboard-Driven Navigation
features.description: Customizable keyboard shortcuts let power users move quickly between feeds and articles without touching the mouse.

features.title: Plugin System
features.description: Extend functionality with community and official plugins, including full-text extraction for feeds that only publish summaries.

features.title: Self-Hosted Privacy
features.description: Runs entirely on infrastructure the user controls, so reading habits and subscriptions are never sent to a third-party service.

features.title: Docker Deployment
features.description: Officially supported Docker and Docker Compose setup makes installation and updates straightforward.

features.title: Official Android Client
features.description: A dedicated Android app lets users read and manage their feeds on mobile devices.

pros: Completely free and open source with no subscription or paid tier
Full data ownership since feeds and reading history stay on the user's own server
Highly customizable through keyboard shortcuts and a plugin ecosystem
Long track record dating back to 2005, with an active community fork continuing development
Official Android client available for mobile reading
cons: Requires self-hosting knowledge, including PHP, a database and Docker, rather than a one-click signup
Recent handover from the original founder to a community fork introduces some uncertainty about long-term direction
Interface feels dated compared to modern commercial feed readers
No official iOS client, relying on third-party apps for Apple devices
Initial setup and plugin configuration can be technical for non-developers

faqs.question: What is Tiny Tiny RSS?
faqs.answer: Tiny Tiny RSS, or tt-rss, is a free, open-source, self-hosted RSS and Atom feed reader written in PHP that lets users aggregate and read feeds from their own server instead of a third-party service.

faqs.question: Is Tiny Tiny RSS free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it is completely free and open source under the GPLv3 license, with no subscription fees.

faqs.question: Who created Tiny Tiny RSS?
faqs.answer: It was originally created by developer Andrew Dolgov, with the project dating back to 2005.

faqs.question: Is Tiny Tiny RSS still maintained?
faqs.answer: The original tt-rss.org project was retired on November 1, 2025. Development continues as a community-run fork on GitHub, created by a long-time contributor in October 2025, aiming to continue the project without major breaking changes.

faqs.question: How do you install Tiny Tiny RSS?
faqs.answer: The recommended installation method is Docker and Docker Compose, though it can also be installed manually on any server running PHP and a supported database.

faqs.question: Does Tiny Tiny RSS have a mobile app?
faqs.answer: Yes, there is an official Android client. iOS users rely on third-party apps that support tt-rss's API.

faqs.question: Can I import my feeds from another RSS reader into tt-rss?
faqs.answer: Yes, tt-rss supports OPML import and export, making it straightforward to migrate subscriptions from readers like Feedly or Inoreader.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Tiny Tiny RSS and TinyFeed?
faqs.answer: They are unrelated projects. Tiny Tiny RSS is a full-featured, self-hosted PHP feed reader and aggregator dating back to 2005, while TinyFeed is a separate, much newer lightweight Go-based CLI tool that generates a static webpage from a list of feeds.

use_cases.title: Privacy-focused feed reading
use_cases.description: Users who do not want their reading habits tracked by a commercial feed reader self-host tt-rss to keep full control of their subscription and reading data.

use_cases.title: High-volume news aggregation for power users
use_cases.description: Users who follow dozens or hundreds of feeds rely on tt-rss's keyboard shortcuts and plugin ecosystem to process a high daily article volume efficiently.
use_cases.audience: Researchers, journalists and heavy RSS users

use_cases.title: Self-hosted homelab service
use_cases.description: Homelab enthusiasts add tt-rss to their Docker Compose stack alongside other self-hosted services as their personal news aggregation layer.

enrichment.short_description: TinyFeed is a free, open-source command-line tool written in Go that generates a lightweight, static HTML webpage from a collection of RSS, Atom and JSON feeds.
enrichment.seo_title: TinyFeed: Free Open-Source Static Feed Reader CLI Tool
enrichment.seo_meta_description: TinyFeed is a free, open-source Go CLI tool that turns RSS, Atom and JSON feeds into a lightweight static webpage. See its features and setup.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: TinyFeed is a free, open-source command-line tool built in Go by developer Sebastien Lovergne, published on GitHub under the handle TheBigRoomXXL starting in September 2023. Rather than functioning as a traditional interactive feed reader application, TinyFeed generates a single lightweight, static HTML webpage from a list of RSS, Atom and JSON feed URLs, using the open-source gofeed parsing library to support all three formats. The project grew out of frustration with existing feed reader options: its creator wanted to consume feeds the way one reads a message board, as a simple, chronologically ordered list of links, rather than through a heavier interactive reader interface with unread counts, folders and complex navigation. Because the output is a static page, TinyFeed sites are extremely lightweight, load quickly, and are fully accessible, and they can be customized with external stylesheets and templates, including built-in support for automatic dark and light themes based on system preference. TinyFeed also supports a daemon mode that periodically regenerates the output page on a schedule, so a self-hosted instance can be left running to always show current feed items without manual intervention. It is released under the MIT license and, as of mid-2026, has accumulated a few hundred stars on GitHub, making it a niche but actively used tool among developers and self-hosters who prefer minimalist, static tooling over heavier feed reader software. TinyFeed has no company or pricing model behind it, is entirely free to use, and should not be confused with the unrelated, much older and more feature-rich Tiny Tiny RSS project, despite the similar name.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is TinyFeed
paragraphs: TinyFeed is a free, open-source command-line tool written in Go that converts a list of RSS, Atom and JSON feeds into a single lightweight, static HTML webpage. It was created by developer Sebastien Lovergne and first published on GitHub in September 2023 under the project name TheBigRoomXXL/tinyfeed.

The tool was built out of dissatisfaction with existing feed reader options. Its creator wanted a way to consume RSS feeds similar to browsing a message board or link aggregator: a simple, time-ordered list of items rather than a heavier interactive reader with folders, unread counters and complex navigation.

Because TinyFeed produces a fully static webpage rather than running a database-backed web application, the output is extremely lightweight, fast to load and accessible, making it well suited to self-hosting on minimal infrastructure or serving from a static file host.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: TinyFeed uses the open-source gofeed library to parse RSS, Atom and JSON feed formats, so a single configuration file listing feed URLs can pull in items from virtually any standard feed source into one combined output page.

The generated page supports automatic dark and light themes based on the visitor's system preference, and the output is fully customizable through external stylesheets and templates, letting users match the page to their own site design.

TinyFeed includes a daemon mode that periodically regenerates the static output page on a set schedule, so a long-running self-hosted instance always reflects current feed items without requiring the user to manually re-run the tool.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: TinyFeed is completely free and open source under the MIT license, with the full source code available on GitHub. There are no paid tiers, subscriptions or usage limits.

Because it produces a static webpage rather than running as a hosted service, the only ongoing cost is whatever minimal hosting the user chooses to serve the generated HTML file from, which can be free static file hosting.

features.title: Multi-Format Feed Support
features.description: Parses RSS, Atom and JSON feeds using the gofeed library so a single tool can combine feeds in different formats into one page.

features.title: Static HTML Output
features.description: Generates a single lightweight, static webpage rather than running a database-backed web application, keeping the footprint minimal.

features.title: Dark and Light Theme Support
features.description: Automatically adapts to the visitor's system theme preference for a comfortable reading experience.

features.title: Customizable Templates and Stylesheets
features.description: Supports external stylesheets and templates so the generated page can be styled to match a personal site or brand.

features.title: Daemon Mode
features.description: Runs continuously to periodically regenerate the output page on a schedule, keeping a self-hosted instance current automatically.

features.title: Lightweight and Accessible
features.description: The static output is designed to be fast-loading and fully accessible, avoiding the overhead of a full interactive reader application.

features.title: Command-Line Interface
features.description: Configured and run entirely from the command line, making it easy to script or integrate into existing self-hosting workflows.

pros: Completely free and open source under the permissive MIT license
Extremely lightweight static output that loads fast and is highly accessible
Simple, message-board-style presentation appeals to users who dislike cluttered feed reader interfaces
Supports RSS, Atom and JSON feeds through a single tool
Daemon mode allows for a hands-off, always-current self-hosted setup
cons: No interactive features like read and unread tracking, folders or search that traditional feed readers offer
Requires command-line comfort to configure and deploy
Smaller community and project maturity compared to established readers like Tiny Tiny RSS or FreshRSS
No official mobile app; the static page must be accessed through a browser
Best suited to a specific minimalist use case rather than general-purpose feed management

faqs.question: What is TinyFeed?
faqs.answer: TinyFeed is a free, open-source command-line tool written in Go that generates a lightweight static webpage from a list of RSS, Atom and JSON feeds.

faqs.question: Is TinyFeed free?
faqs.answer: Yes, TinyFeed is completely free and open source under the MIT license.

faqs.question: Who created TinyFeed?
faqs.answer: TinyFeed was created by developer Sebastien Lovergne and first published on GitHub in September 2023 under the handle TheBigRoomXXL.

faqs.question: How is TinyFeed different from a traditional RSS reader?
faqs.answer: Instead of running an interactive web application with folders and unread counts, TinyFeed generates a single static HTML page listing feed items in chronological order, similar to a message board.

faqs.question: Does TinyFeed support Atom and JSON feeds, or just RSS?
faqs.answer: TinyFeed supports RSS, Atom and JSON feeds using the open-source gofeed parsing library.

faqs.question: Can TinyFeed update automatically?
faqs.answer: Yes, TinyFeed includes a daemon mode that periodically regenerates the static output page on a schedule so the page stays current without manual re-runs.

faqs.question: Is TinyFeed the same as Tiny Tiny RSS?
faqs.answer: No, they are unrelated projects. Tiny Tiny RSS is a much older, full-featured, self-hosted PHP feed reader dating back to 2005, while TinyFeed is a newer, minimalist Go-based tool that outputs a static webpage.

faqs.question: Does TinyFeed have a mobile app?
faqs.answer: No, TinyFeed does not have a dedicated mobile app; the generated static page is accessed through any web browser, including on mobile devices.

use_cases.title: Minimalist self-hosted feed page
use_cases.description: Individuals who want a simple, distraction-free way to browse their favorite feeds deploy TinyFeed to generate a single static page instead of running a full feed reader application.

use_cases.title: Lightweight feed page for a personal website
use_cases.description: Bloggers and personal site owners embed a TinyFeed-generated page into their own site to showcase a curated list of feeds they follow.

use_cases.title: Low-resource feed aggregation on constrained infrastructure
use_cases.description: Users running services on minimal hardware, like a Raspberry Pi or a small VPS, use TinyFeed because its static output avoids the overhead of a database-backed reader application.

features: Full source code under MIT license

enrichment.short_description: Tipalti is a global finance automation platform that streamlines accounts payable, mass payments, procurement, expenses and tax compliance for finance teams paying suppliers and partners in multiple currencies worldwide.
enrichment.seo_title: Tipalti Review 2026: Pricing, Features and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Tipalti automates accounts payable, mass payments and procurement for global finance teams. Compare Tipalti pricing, features and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Tipalti is a finance automation platform that helps mid-market and enterprise companies manage accounts payable, global mass payments, procurement and employee expenses from one connected system. It was founded in 2010 by Chen Amit, who serves as CEO, and Oren Zeev, and is headquartered in Foster City, California, with additional offices including Amsterdam, London, Plano, Tbilisi, Tel Aviv, Toronto and Vancouver. The company built its reputation on solving payables and payouts for platforms with large numbers of suppliers, freelancers or partners around the world, supporting payments in roughly 190 countries and dozens of currencies and payment methods including wire transfer, PayPal, ACH and local bank transfers, while automating tax compliance tasks like W-9 and W-8 collection. Tipalti has raised several hundred million dollars in funding, reached unicorn status following a Series E round, and was valued at multiple billions of dollars in subsequent rounds and debt financing, growing to well over a thousand employees globally. Its product suite includes Accounts Payable automation, covering invoice capture, approval workflows and payment execution, Mass Payments for high-volume global payouts to marketplaces, ad networks and creator platforms, Procurement for purchase order and spend management, Expenses for employee expense management, and Tipalti Card, a corporate card with cashback on invoice payments. Pricing is subscription-based with usage layered on top: published starting prices are around 99 US dollars per month for the Accounts Payable product and around 249 US dollars per month for Mass Payments, with the final cost depending on transaction volume, number of legal entities, enabled modules and payment geography; Tipalti does not charge per-user or per-approver fees, and there is no free tier. It competes with Bill.com, AvidXchange, Airbase, Ramp and Trolley (Payment Rails), differentiating itself primarily through global payment reach and tax-compliance automation for platforms that pay a large volume of external payees rather than only internal vendors.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Tipalti
paragraphs: Tipalti is a finance automation platform built for mid-market and enterprise finance teams that need to manage accounts payable, global payouts, procurement and employee expenses from a single connected system. The company was founded in 2010 by Chen Amit, who serves as CEO, and Oren Zeev, and is headquartered in Foster City, California, with offices across North America, Europe and the Middle East.

Tipalti built its early reputation specifically around solving mass payouts for platforms and marketplaces that need to pay large numbers of external suppliers, freelancers, affiliates or creators around the world, a problem that traditional accounts payable software was not designed to handle at scale. It supports payments in roughly 190 countries across dozens of currencies and payment methods, and automates the tax documentation, such as W-9 and W-8BEN forms, required for compliant cross-border payouts.

The company has grown into a well-funded, large-scale finance technology company, having raised several hundred million dollars in funding, reached unicorn status, and expanded to well over a thousand employees globally as of the mid-2020s.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Tipalti's Accounts Payable module automates invoice capture, multi-level approval workflows and payment execution, reducing the manual work finance teams spend chasing approvals and reconciling payments. Its self-service supplier portal lets payees manage their own banking details and tax forms rather than requiring the finance team to collect that information manually.

The Mass Payments product is built for platforms, marketplaces, ad networks and creator economy businesses that need to pay large volumes of external payees globally. It supports numerous payment methods and currencies, batch payment processing, and automated tax compliance so businesses can scale payouts without proportionally scaling headcount.

Additional modules extend Tipalti into Procurement, covering purchase requests, approvals and purchase order management, Expenses for employee expense management and reimbursement, and Tipalti Card, a corporate card program that offers cashback on invoice payments, letting finance teams consolidate more of their payment stack in one platform.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Tipalti uses a subscription pricing model with usage-based elements layered on top. Published starting prices are roughly 99 US dollars per month for Accounts Payable and roughly 249 US dollars per month for Mass Payments, both including unlimited users since Tipalti does not charge per-user or per-approver fees.

Final pricing depends on transaction volume, the number of legal entities being managed, which modules are enabled, and the global payment methods and currencies required, and there is no free tier. Mid-market and enterprise customers typically receive a custom quote after a consultation with Tipalti's sales team.

features.title: Invoice Capture and Approval Workflows
features.description: Automates invoice intake and routes approvals through configurable, multi-level workflows to reduce manual accounts payable work.

features.title: Global Mass Payments
features.description: Pays large volumes of suppliers, freelancers or partners across roughly 190 countries using multiple currencies and payment methods.

features.title: Self-Service Supplier Portal
features.description: Lets payees manage their own banking details and tax documentation, reducing manual data collection for finance teams.

features.title: Automated Tax Compliance
features.description: Collects and validates tax forms like W-9 and W-8BEN and helps generate required tax reporting for cross-border payouts.

features.title: Procurement Management
features.description: Manages purchase requests, approvals and purchase orders alongside the broader payables workflow.

features.title: Employee Expense Management
features.description: Handles employee expense submission, approval and reimbursement within the same platform as vendor payments.

features.title: Tipalti Card
features.description: A corporate card program offering cashback on invoice payments to help offset platform costs.

features.title: ERP Integrations
features.description: Connects with major ERP and accounting systems to keep payment and financial data synchronized.

pros: Strong global payment reach across roughly 190 countries and many currencies and payment methods
No per-user or per-approver fees, so adding team members does not increase license cost
Automates tax compliance work that is otherwise time-consuming for cross-border payouts
Combines accounts payable, mass payments, procurement and expenses in one connected suite
Well-funded, established company with a long operating history since 2010
cons: No free tier, and starting prices are aimed at mid-market and enterprise budgets rather than small businesses
Full pricing requires a custom quote, making upfront cost comparison harder
Implementation and onboarding can take longer than lightweight AP tools aimed at smaller companies
Best suited to companies with high payment volume or many payees, so it may be more platform than very small businesses need
Some modules, including Procurement, Expenses and Tipalti Card, are priced and sold separately from core Accounts Payable

faqs.question: What is Tipalti used for?
faqs.answer: Tipalti is used by finance teams to automate accounts payable, global mass payments, procurement and employee expenses, particularly for businesses that pay large numbers of suppliers or partners internationally.

faqs.question: How much does Tipalti cost?
faqs.answer: Tipalti's Accounts Payable product starts at roughly 99 US dollars per month and Mass Payments starts at roughly 249 US dollars per month, with final pricing depending on transaction volume, legal entities and enabled modules; there is no free tier.

faqs.question: Who founded Tipalti?
faqs.answer: Tipalti was founded in 2010 by Chen Amit, who serves as CEO, and Oren Zeev.

faqs.question: Where is Tipalti headquartered?
faqs.answer: Tipalti is headquartered in Foster City, California, with additional offices in cities including Amsterdam, London, Plano, Tbilisi, Tel Aviv, Toronto and Vancouver.

faqs.question: Does Tipalti charge per user?
faqs.answer: No, Tipalti does not charge per-user or per-approver fees; pricing is based on transaction volume and enabled modules instead.

faqs.question: How many countries does Tipalti support payments in?
faqs.answer: Tipalti supports global payouts in roughly 190 countries across multiple currencies and payment methods.

faqs.question: What is Tipalti Mass Payments used for?
faqs.answer: Mass Payments is designed for platforms, marketplaces, ad networks and creator economy businesses that need to pay large volumes of external payees around the world.

faqs.question: What are Tipalti's main competitors?
faqs.answer: Tipalti competes with accounts payable and payment automation platforms including Bill.com, AvidXchange, Airbase, Ramp and Trolley (Payment Rails).

use_cases.title: Global supplier and vendor payments
use_cases.description: Finance teams use Tipalti's Accounts Payable module to automate invoice approval and payment for a large base of domestic and international suppliers.

use_cases.title: Marketplace and platform payouts
use_cases.description: Marketplaces, ad networks and creator platforms use Tipalti Mass Payments to pay thousands of freelancers, sellers or creators globally while automating tax compliance.

use_cases.title: Consolidated payables and procurement
use_cases.description: Companies use Tipalti's combined Accounts Payable and Procurement modules to manage purchasing approvals and vendor payments within a single connected workflow.
use_cases.audience: Finance and procurement teams at growing companies

enrichment.short_description: Tito is a fast, design-focused event registration and ticketing platform used by conference and event organizers to sell tickets, manage attendees and handle check-in, charging a simple per-ticket fee instead of a monthly subscription.
enrichment.seo_title: Tito Review 2026: Pricing, Features and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Tito is a per-ticket-fee event registration platform. See Tito's pricing, features, check-in tools and how it compares to Eventbrite and other alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Tito is an event registration and ticketing platform built by Team Tito, a small, self-funded, design-led software company based in Dublin, Ireland. It began as a side project started by developer Paul Campbell in 2012, reportedly while working alongside Paddy Cosgrave, later the founder of the Web Summit conference series, and moved into public beta in April 2013 after processing an initial private beta workload of over one million US dollars in ticket sales. Tito has since processed more than one billion US dollars in ticket sales for organizers worldwide. The platform lets event organizers create registration pages, sell tickets, collect custom attendee information, manage waitlists and discount codes, run check-in on event day, and export financial reports, positioning itself as a faster, more flexible and less cluttered alternative to larger platforms like Eventbrite. Rather than charging a monthly subscription or platform fee, Tito uses a simple usage-based pricing model: creating an account and running a free event costs nothing, and paid events are charged a 3 percent fee per ticket sold, reduced to roughly 2.5 percent for qualifying charity, non-profit or community events, with a fee cap of 25 euros per ticket for higher-priced tickets. Ticket buyers pay through Stripe or PayPal, which charge their own separate transaction processing fees on top of Tito's platform fee, and payouts are typically deposited to the organizer's bank account within a few business days of the sale via the connected payment processor. Tito supports payment processing in 139 currencies, making it usable by organizers in most countries where Stripe or PayPal operate. It is frequently chosen by tech conferences, meetups and community events that value a clean, fast checkout experience and transparent, pay-as-you-sell pricing over a flat monthly software fee.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Tito
paragraphs: Tito is an event registration and ticketing platform built by Team Tito, a small, self-funded, design-led software company based in Dublin, Ireland. The product began as a side project started by developer Paul Campbell in 2012, moving into public beta in April 2013 after its private beta had already processed more than a million US dollars in ticket sales, and has since processed over a billion US dollars in tickets for organizers around the world.

Tito positions itself as a faster, cleaner and more design-focused alternative to larger ticketing platforms like Eventbrite, aimed particularly at tech conferences, meetups and community-run events that want a streamlined checkout experience without heavy branding or clutter from the ticketing provider.

As a bootstrapped, independently owned company, Tito has grown steadily without outside venture funding, building a reputation among conference organizers for reliability, responsive support and a straightforward, transparent pricing model rather than a large feature-bloated platform.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Tito lets organizers build a customizable registration page, sell multiple ticket types with different prices and quantities, set up discount codes and waitlists, and collect custom attendee information through configurable registration questions, all without requiring buyers to create an account.

On event day, Tito provides check-in tools, including a mobile-friendly check-in interface and badge or ticket scanning support, so organizers can manage attendee arrival without a separate check-in product.

The platform includes built-in financial reporting and CSV export, integrates with payment processors Stripe and PayPal to collect payments in 139 currencies, and connects to common marketing and productivity tools so ticket and attendee data can flow into an organizer's existing workflow.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Tito uses a usage-based pricing model rather than a monthly subscription. There is no cost to create an account or run a free event; paid events are charged a 3 percent fee on each ticket sold, reduced to roughly 2.5 percent for qualifying charity, non-profit or community events after application, with no setup fee or charge for using any premium features.

Tito caps its per-ticket platform fee at 25 euros for higher-priced tickets, so events with expensive tickets do not pay an unbounded percentage fee. Ticket buyers' payments are processed through Stripe or PayPal, which charge their own separate transaction fees, for example around 2.9 percent plus 30 cents in the United States, in addition to Tito's platform fee.

features.title: Customizable Registration Pages
features.description: Build branded event registration pages with multiple ticket types, pricing tiers and quantities.

features.title: Discount Codes and Waitlists
features.description: Create discount codes for promotions and automatically manage waitlists when ticket types sell out.

features.title: Custom Attendee Questions
features.description: Collect custom information from ticket buyers through configurable registration questions without requiring account creation.

features.title: Event-Day Check-In
features.description: Mobile-friendly check-in tools with ticket or badge scanning to manage attendee arrival on the day of the event.

features.title: Financial Reporting and Export
features.description: Built-in reporting and CSV export make it easy to reconcile ticket sales and revenue.

features.title: Stripe and PayPal Integration
features.description: Accept payments in 139 currencies through connected Stripe or PayPal accounts, with funds paid directly to the organizer's bank account.

features.title: Third-Party Integrations
features.description: Connects with marketing, CRM and productivity tools so attendee and ticket data can flow into an organizer's existing systems.

pros: No monthly subscription fee; organizers only pay when tickets actually sell
Clean, fast checkout experience with less clutter than many larger ticketing platforms
Fee is capped at 25 euros per ticket, protecting organizers of high-priced events
Reduced fee available for qualifying charity, non-profit and community events
Long track record and strong reputation specifically within the tech conference and community-event space
cons: Per-ticket fee can add up for very large events compared to a flat subscription model
Requires connecting a separate Stripe or PayPal account, which adds its own transaction fees
Smaller company than major competitors like Eventbrite, with a correspondingly smaller marketplace and discovery audience
Feature set is more focused on registration and check-in than broader event marketing or venue-management tools
No built-in ticket marketplace or discovery traffic the way larger platforms provide

faqs.question: What is Tito used for?
faqs.answer: Tito is used by event organizers to create registration pages, sell tickets, collect attendee information, manage waitlists and discount codes, and check in attendees on the day of an event.

faqs.question: How much does Tito cost?
faqs.answer: Tito has no monthly subscription. Free events cost nothing to run, and paid events are charged a 3 percent fee per ticket sold, reduced to roughly 2.5 percent for qualifying non-profit or charity events, with a fee cap of 25 euros per ticket.

faqs.question: Who founded Tito?
faqs.answer: Tito began as a side project started by developer Paul Campbell in 2012 and is built by Team Tito, a bootstrapped company based in Dublin, Ireland.

faqs.question: Does Tito charge a setup fee?
faqs.answer: No, Tito has no setup fee, no monthly subscription and no extra charge to use premium features; organizers only pay the per-ticket fee on paid tickets sold.

faqs.question: What payment processors does Tito support?
faqs.answer: Tito integrates with Stripe and PayPal to collect payments, supporting 139 currencies, and both processors charge their own separate transaction fees.

faqs.question: How is Tito different from Eventbrite?
faqs.answer: Tito focuses on a faster, more design-forward checkout experience aimed largely at tech conferences and community events, while Eventbrite is a larger, more marketing- and discovery-oriented ticketing marketplace.

faqs.question: Does Tito offer discounted pricing for non-profits?
faqs.answer: Yes, qualifying charity, non-profit and community events can apply for a reduced fee of roughly 2.5 percent instead of the standard 3 percent.

faqs.question: Does Tito have event check-in tools?
faqs.answer: Yes, Tito includes mobile-friendly check-in tools with ticket or badge scanning for managing attendee arrival on event day.

use_cases.title: Tech conference ticketing
use_cases.description: Conference organizers use Tito to sell multiple ticket tiers, manage waitlists and check attendees in quickly at the door.

use_cases.title: Community meetup registration
use_cases.description: Meetup and community group organizers use Tito's free tier to register attendees for no-cost events without any platform fee.

use_cases.title: Non-profit and charity event ticketing
use_cases.description: Non-profits use Tito's reduced fee rate for charity events to keep more ticket revenue going toward their cause.

features: Fee capped at EUR 25 per ticket
Same feature set as paid plan
Fee capped at EUR 25 per ticket
